lines.name: Ising Game
lines.description: Open with the top-middle of the center mini-board (C:T). You play on the meta center without taking C:C, and your opponent’s next move is locked to the top meta—useful when you want an early vertical lane instead of the deepest center duel.

lines.name: Standard meta center
lines.description: After C:C on the meta center, O grabs a corner on that board and X takes the center of the mini-board they get sent to. You contest the inherited grid symmetrically and avoid handing over easy center-and-corner pairs on the same local board.

lines.name: Meta center rim reply
lines.description: Same C:C on the meta center plus a corner reply there, but X plays an outer-edge cell on the board they inherit—fighting along the rim builds edge threats and keeps the local center open longer than taking TL:C immediately.

lines.name: Same-side rails
lines.description: O hits a middle-side on C (not a corner), X continues along that same facing rail on the meta they’re sent to (e.g. T:T). You stack influence along one axis across boards so sends stay correlated instead of scattering.

lines.name: Outside meta center entry
lines.description: Anchor a corner meta with TL:C (etc.), pull O into C:C, then step into the meta center from your corner sector. You force an early fight on the meta center while keeping an outside meta already developed.

lines.name: Stacked corners
lines.description: After C:C and C:TL, X answers TL:TL—same corner type relative to each mini-board. Both sides keep doubling down in one wedge so the whole early game stays channelled through a single meta corridor.

lines.name: Inward corner meta center
lines.description: X opens with the deepest corner on a corner meta-board, meets the center of that board on move two, then touches the global center. Strength is situational; the pattern is easy to teach.

lines.name: Far-corner on meta center
lines.description: TL:C then C:C, then X hits the far-type corner on C (e.g. C:BR). Instead of mirroring the standard meta center line, you stretch threats across the center mini so O can’t solve one diagonal template.

lines.name: Corner meta center flank
lines.description: TL:TL → TL:C then exit through a side cell on C (e.g. C:L). You touch three metas quickly with mixed-depth moves—corner stack, then center local, then a lateral send—so the position stays tactically mixed.

lines.name: Cross rim reply
lines.description: Same first two moves as Meta center rim reply (C:C, corner on C), but the third move is a perpendicular edge (e.g. TL:R not TL:T). You still fight the inherited mini from the outside band but turn threats across the board instead of lining them along one rim.
